ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Sprite; 'carbonated beverage' (FOODON_03301638) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN000A (Soft drinks) is the closest parent category; no Sprite-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 'Carbonated soft drink' is the closest parent; no brand-specific Sprite code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of Sprite is 330 ml (approximately 330 g), which is the typical restaurant and retail serving size in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Sprite, lemon-lime flavored carbonated soft drink — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 14148)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Sprite lemon-lime carbonated soft drink used as primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g of beverage.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, Sprite (Coca-Cola) — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sprite has a published GI of 63 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 330 ml serving calculated as: (63 × 10.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.3) / 100 = approximately 22.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (7 mg per 100 g); however, high sugar content may contribute to weight gain which is an indirect risk factor for hypertension.
hypertension.guidance: Sprite is low in sodium and poses minimal direct hypertensive risk, but regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with weight gain and should be limited in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (9.7 g per 100 g; approximately 32 g per 330 ml can) and medium glycemic index (GI 63) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Sprite due to its high free sugar load and medium GI, which can cause significant postprandial hyperglycaemia; sugar-free alternatives or water are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content linked to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and increased cardiovascular risk with habitual consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages like Sprite is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; patients with CVD should minimise intake and opt for unsweetened beverages.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sprite is a globally recognised clear, carbonated lemon-lime flavoured soft drink manufactured by The Coca-Cola Company. It is sold in cans, glass bottles, and plastic bottles and is widely available across Singapore restaurants, hawker centres, and convenience stores. The beverage is characterised by its crisp, refreshing citrus flavour derived from natural lemon and lime flavourings, citric acid, and sodium citrate, combined with carbonated water and sugar (or high-fructose corn syrup depending on market). In Singapore, Sprite is commonly served as a standalone cold drink or as a mixer. It contains no protein, no fat, and no dietary fibre; its caloric content derives entirely from free sugars (approximately 9.7 g per 100 g, or about 32 g per standard 330 ml can). With a glycemic index of 63 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 22 per can, Sprite poses a significant glycaemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed sparingly. It is not a source of vitamins or minerals of nutritional significance. The Chinese name 雪碧 (Xuě Bì) is widely used across Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore and China. Sprite is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and contains no common allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of Sprite?
What is the sugar content of Sprite?
What is the GI of Sprite?
What is 雪碧 in English?
Can I drink Sprite if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Sprite lemon-lime carbonated soft drink (an exact product match). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database with an exact product match. All mandatory multilingual names for Singapore context are present.
notes: Sprite is a branded carbonated soft drink; nutrient values are per 100 g of beverage as reported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. protein_g is confirmed zero — contains no protein by product formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero — contains no fat by product formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are confirmed zero by product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by product formulation. All vitamin and mineral fields reported as 0.0 are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (negligible or not detected). selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for this product. glycemic_index is 63 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database (exact match). Glycemic load per 330 ml serving = (63 × 10.6 × 3.3) / 100 ≈ 22.0. The cuisine field is set to 'Global / International' as Sprite is a globally manufactured branded product, not a cuisine-specific dish; the input cuisine tag 'Chinese' reflects the restaurant context in which it is served in Singapore, not the product's origin. Multilingual names are provided for Singapore context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. The food_id GD000405 is preserved from the input record.
